I mean, none of them cracked. None of them. The turtle, she's got a perfect straight face while she's selling that turtle. That doesn't happen without practice. I mean, you can't just walk in here and do that. You know, you had to work at that. And the water thing, the water thing is, they're in a fancy restaurant. There's a wine steward. There's a menu. It must be real, right? And of course, he kept a straight face too. I mean, that's our business. That's it. That's what we do. That's it. And all of those examples are well worth careful and closer study. Well, let's talk about money a little bit. You were promised a little bonus session on that. And I pulled some stuff for you. And we'll start here. A lot of the attraction of money from people, the getting of money from people, has to do with how you think and feel about money. And it maybe has as much to do with that. It has anything else. And move this away from me. And so there's sort of two overall overriding philosophies about money. One is that there is a finite amount of it. And that if you subtract some, the subtraction is permanent. And that if you get a dollar, it had to diminish someone else's store of dollars. In business, one of the reasons corporate clients are frustrating is two things. One is their philosophy about money includes the idea of budgets. So they develop a advertising budget for the year in advance of the year. Well, what's wrong with that picture? It's a predetermined restriction of how much their sales can be for the entire year because they based it on a formulaic equation against their sales. So when really your advertising budget should be as much as you can possibly spend as fast as you can turn the money over. So sell more, advertise more, sell more, advertise more. Who knows how much you should spend on advertising this year? Because we don't know how much you're going to sell this year yet. Their budgeting is based on the idea of finite dollars. The other corporate thing that gets talked about is market share. And so everybody starts out with a predetermined idea that there's a size of a market. And so there's only so many people who are going to do college planning this year. And so there's only so many people who are going to get cosmetic dentistry this year. There's only so many people who are going to get coaching this year. There's only so many people. And it all adds up to this. And now we're all fighting and worrying over our share of that market. But what happens if we can make the market bigger? Then all bets are off, right? In many respects, I was sorry to see him get canned. The last new CEO of Home Depot, the recently departed CEO of Home Depot, made the excellent point that Home Depot's valuations, everybody's opinion about Home Depot, was based on market share. But his argument was it's based on the do-it-yourself market. But the fastest growing and evolving market is the do-it-for-them market, not the do-it-yourself market. So what happens if we combine that market with the do-it-yourself market, and then there's the contractor market, which we haven't done a very good job with. So what happens if we put that in there? So now all the market share measurements are off. So when you think in these limited terms, you automatically set yourself up for limited results. And so a lot of people believe that there's a finite amount of money. So each customer or client has a finite amount of money that they are going to spend in a particular category in a year. And then what share of that are you going to get? Each customer or client has a finite amount of money that they're going to spend total.
